rounds.blurb: "Into the Jaws of Death": U.S. troops wade toward Omaha Beach on the morning of D-Day.
rounds.credit: Robert F. Sargent, U.S. Coast Guard — public domain
rounds.story: Coast Guard coxswain Robert Sargent shot this from the ramp of his landing craft at Omaha Beach on D-Day morning, delivering the 1st Infantry Division into machine-gun fire. By nightfall the Allies had put more than 150,000 men ashore.

rounds.blurb: The Women's March on Washington, the day after the presidential inauguration.
rounds.story: With sister marches on every continent — including a boat off Antarctica — the day drew an estimated four million participants in the U.S. alone, likely the largest single-day protest in American history.

rounds.blurb: UH-1D helicopters lift infantry during Operation Wahiawa, northeast of Cu Chi, South Vietnam.
rounds.credit: James K. F. Dung, SFC, U.S. Army — public domain
rounds.story: The Huey's rotor thump became the signature sound of the war; more than 7,000 served in Vietnam and roughly half never came home. This lift was part of a 25th Infantry Division sweep near Cu Chi, above an immense Viet Cong tunnel network.

rounds.blurb: Space Shuttle Atlantis docked to Russia's Mir station, photographed from a departing Soyuz.
rounds.story: The first shuttle-Mir docking briefly created the largest spacecraft ever assembled — and this photo exists only because a departing Soyuz backed off to take it. The handshake between rival programs led directly to the ISS.
keywords: a shuttle docked to a Russian station

rounds.blurb: Perseverance's selfie with the Ingenuity helicopter at Jezero Crater, Mars.
rounds.story: Perseverance posed with Ingenuity, a four-pound helicopter that days later made the first powered flight on another planet — carrying a swatch of the Wright Flyer's wing fabric. Rated for five flights, it flew 72.
